segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Richard Bernstein from Richard Bernstein Advisors, a bald man in glasses and a gray shirt in a home office, stating, "I personally think we are right in maybe the biggest bubble of my career." This transitions to a woman in a striped top in a cozy room with bookshelves, who explains, "Once the Fed came in, people now expect the Fed's going to come in again." Next, a man in a blue shirt and tie in a professional setting with a globe discusses the "big challenge is raising interest rates enough without tipping the economy into a recession." A close-up of hands counting U.S. dollar bills precedes a woman with white hair in a professional office reviewing a tablet, asserting that "People's expectations, their views of the future inflation actually results in higher inflation. That's the problem." A man in a dark suit and glasses in a home setting then critically observes, "When you have a Federal Reserve that one cycle after another, every time they have a crisis hit, they try and solve the problem of over-indebtedness by putting more debt into the economy." Following this, a man in a suit and tie at a podium declares, "You look at the central bank balance sheets exploding right now, and you say, there's going to be inflation." A bell rings, introducing a sustained, slightly ominous synth sound as a trader on the New York Stock Exchange floor is shown focused on financial screens. A woman in a red top appears in front of a 'Quill Intelligence' backdrop while a narrator states, "Stock market observers are sounding an alarm." Richard Bernstein's voice returns, reiterating his "biggest bubble" comment, as the Federal Reserve building's exterior and engraved facade are shown, followed by a woman with glasses in front of a world map. The scene then shifts to an aerial view of a suburban neighborhood, a title card 'How the U.S. tries to control inflation' over currency printing and stock charts, and a bustling NYSE floor, while a new male voice notes, "Investors have loaded up on risky assets like housing..." The segment concludes with Richard Bernstein again, now with a timestamp, and a final shot of the Peloton building with workers outside.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of golden Bitcoin coins, as a male voiceover mentions 'tech stocks and even cryptocurrency.' Jerome Powell, Chair of the Federal Reserve, then speaks at the Federal Open Market Committee Conference on December 15, 2021, stating in a calm, authoritative voice that 'asset valuations are somewhat elevated' and 'cryptocurrencies... are really speculative assets... risky, they're not backed by anything.' A male voiceover then explains that 'market problems started at the top US bank, the Federal Reserve,' as the scene shifts to the dynamic NYSE trading floor with digital screens displaying market data. Automated machinery processes stacks of U.S. dollar bills, followed by a person counting cash, while the voiceover details how 'the Fed controls all of the money in circulation,' including 'money in your wallet and the coffers at banks,' and 'can print more during financial emergencies.' A Woman in a striped top, seated in a home office, then speaks directly to the camera in a clear, conversational voice, noting, 'Once the Fed came in, people now expect the Fed's going to come in again.' An exterior shot of the Federal Reserve building appears with a graphic overlay showing the 'Effective Federal Funds Rate, Feb 2022' at .08%, as the male voiceover continues, explaining that 'for the last two decades, the US Central Bank has kept interest rates on loans as cheap as possible.' A close-up of U.S. dollar bills is followed by a line graph titled 'Federal Reserve System balance sheet trends' from 2001 to 2021, showing a sharp increase, which the voiceover attributes to the Fed buying bonds and flooding the market with 'emergency cash,' causing the balance to 'crescendoed to nearly $9 trillion, an all-time high.' Roger W. Ferguson Jr., Former Vice Chair of the Federal Reserve, then speaks in a professional setting, articulating that 'the balance sheet has become more of a tool of policy,' and 'the Federal Reserve is using its balance sheet to drive better outcomes.' The segment concludes with a group celebrating on a balcony at the NYSE, ringing the closing bell on October 25, 2021, as the male voiceover states, 'The Fed's actions led the market to historic highs.'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of U.S. Treasury bonds as a male voiceover states the belief that this bond-buying program needs to end, emphasizing "the sooner the better." This transitions to an automated money processing machine handling stacks of $100 bills, with the text '$2-3 trillion' overlaid, as the voiceover projects "a two to three trillion dollar wind down in the Fed's bond portfolio over coming years." The scene then shows the iconic Charging Bull statue in New York City, symbolizing market strength, while the voiceover suggests this action "would stabilize markets." The focus shifts to the New York Stock Exchange (NYSE) trading floor, where digital screens display stock tickers like 'Verizon' and 'Walmart' with fluctuating prices, as the voiceover warns of "a risk" that if "the Fed drops its emergency stimulus too quickly, it could spark a recession." Greg McBride, identified as Senior Vice President and Chief Financial Analyst at Bankrate.com, appears in a professional home office setting, speaking clearly and articulately about "the big challenge" of "raising interest rates enough, tightening policy enough to corral inflation without tipping the economy into a recession," concluding it's "easier said than done." A dramatic red-toned graphic then poses the question, 'Can the Fed sell its assets without starting a recession?' against a backdrop of falling stock market data, with McBride adding that "history is not necessarily on their side." The Federal Reserve building is then shown, with text listing its assets, as a different male voice asks how "the Fed acquire[d] nearly nine trillion dollars worth of assets" and if "they sell them without breaking the economy." This voice continues, explaining that "The US government relies on its central bank, the Federal Reserve, to manage the economy." The segment concludes with a historical black-and-white image of Wall Street during the '1907 Banker's Panic,' with a dense crowd gathered outside the New York Stock Exchange, as the second male voice explains that "The Federal Reserve itself was created after a major crisis," specifically "a financial crisis in 1907," when "we didn't have a central bank," leading to a study that concluded the need to avoid "future crises."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a historical black-and-white image of Wall Street during the '1907 Banker's Panic,' showing a dense crowd outside the New York Stock Exchange, as a male voice discusses the need for a central bank to "create more currency during times of stress." The scene transitions to a modern-day exterior shot of the Federal Reserve building, followed by a Woman in a striped top, who, speaking directly to the camera from a well-lit room with bookshelves, asserts that "The Federal Reserve has proven itself repeatedly over time, well-positioned to be the first responder in the face of any type of shock." A line graph titled 'Federal funds effective rate' then appears, illustrating the rate's decline from over 20% in 1980 to near 0% by 2020, with a blue circle highlighting the 2020 drop, while another male voice explains that "The Fed's most important tool is the federal funds interest rate." Subsequent male voices elaborate, stating the Fed funds rate is "between zero and a quarter percent," which is "as low as they can go," and "highly unusual to have interest rates close to zero." One speaker clarifies that "what the Fed does is it sets the rate at which banks borrow money between themselves overnight," and that "from that short-term rate comes all the other rates that people pay for in terms of mortgage rates or home equity line of credit rates or automobile loan rates," concluding that "all rates are set by banks and by the market based off of that short-term overnight rate that the Federal Reserve sets." The video then cuts to the exterior of a Citibank building at night, followed by a daytime shot of the J.P. Morgan building, as a male voice notes that "Central banks around the world have kept interest rates low to stimulate further growth in the face of unusual financial conditions." Roger W. Ferguson Jr. appears next, speaking in a professional setting, as the voice continues, "In the US, bankers have resisted..." The narrative shifts to a suburban home with a 'SOLD' sign, then to a real estate transaction where keys are exchanged, visually connecting the discussion to real-world impacts. Roger W. Ferguson Jr. reappears, continuing his commentary. The video also shows a Japanese flag waving, a 'CLOSED DUE TO COVID-19' sign on a storefront, and a close-up of the Wall Street street sign with American flags in the background, emphasizing the financial district's current state during the pandemic.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a United States Treasury stimulus check, featuring the Statue of Liberty and the words 'RELIEF PROGRAM' against an American flag backdrop. Roger W. Ferguson Jr., a bald man with glasses wearing a maroon sweater over a white shirt and tie, speaks from a professional setting with a bookshelf behind him, explaining how economic stimulus is delivered through tools like bond portfolios and tracked with a balance sheet. He elaborates that banks have balance sheets with assets and liabilities, identifying currency in circulation (Federal Reserve notes) as a primary liability, which the Federal Reserve backs with assets such as government securities and mortgages. The scene transitions to close-ups of U.S. $100 bills, highlighting the Federal Reserve seal and Benjamin Franklin’s portrait, followed by an automated money processing machine rapidly handling stacks of dollar bills. A narrator's voice explains that the Fed has the power to create more money when the financial system breaks down, leading experts to call it the 'lender of last resort,' a phrase that appears as on-screen text. The Woman in striped top, with dark hair, seated in a room with bookshelves and a couch, then speaks directly to the camera, stating that the 'lender of last resort' was in many ways the original function of the Federal Reserve. She explains that a central bank was initially unwanted due to concerns about aggregating too much power, but this function became necessary. Her commentary continues as the video shows a close-up of the original Federal Reserve Act document, with highlighted text detailing its purpose to establish Federal Reserve banks, furnish elastic currency, and supervise banking, before returning to the Woman in striped top to conclude the segment.
segments.visual_original_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a United States Treasury stimulus check, featuring the Statue of Liberty and the words 'RELIEF PROGRAM,' set against a backdrop of the American flag. The scene transitions to Roger W. Ferguson Jr., a bald man with glasses wearing a maroon sweater over a white shirt and tie, speaking in a professional setting with a bookshelf behind him. The video then cuts to close-ups of U.S. $100 bills, highlighting the Federal Reserve seal and Benjamin Franklin’s portrait, followed by a shot of an automated money processing machine rapidly handling stacks of dollar bills. On-screen text appears, reading 'Lender of last resort,' as the machine continues its operation. The scene shifts to the Woman in striped top, seated in a room with bookshelves and a couch, speaking directly to the camera. This is followed by a close-up of the original Federal Reserve Act document, with the highlighted text 'To provide for the establishment of Federal reserve banks, to furnish an elastic currency, to afford means of rediscounting commercial paper, to establish a more effective supervision of banking in the United States, and for other purposes.' The video returns to the Woman in striped top, who continues her commentary, maintaining the scholarly and informative tone of the segment.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a black-and-white exterior shot of the Federal Reserve building, as a female voice discusses the need for "oversight and accountability." Text overlays appear, listing 'Mortgage-backed securities,' 'Corporate bonds,' and 'Assets and liabilities.' A male narrator then explains that for much of its history, the Federal Reserve "did not make much use of the balance sheet." This transitions to a dark blue line graph titled 'Federal Reserve System balance sheet trends,' sourced from the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis. Another male voice notes that on "9/11, it was a balance sheet of roughly 750, 800 billion dollars," the largest it had ever been. The narrator continues, mentioning Dr. Ferguson leaving the Central Bank before the 2008 "housing crash," which is visually represented by a suburban house with a 'FORECLOSURE' sign. As "nervous investors" pulled out of the market, a busy NYSE trading floor is shown. The graph reappears with an animated arrow emphasizing a sharp rise after 2009, as the narrator explains that Federal Reserve Chair Ben Bernanke "authorized a large-scale purchase of bonds, sending the balance sheet rapidly upward." A rapid montage of multiple voices then repeatedly states "quantitative easing." This leads to a split-screen showing Richard Bernstein discussing Bitcoin’s price drop and Treasury yields, while the female voice from the beginning defines "quantitative easing" as a mechanism "to spur more credit creation" by "buying up safe instruments, Treasuries and agency mortgage-backed securities." This explanation is delivered by the Woman in striped top, seated in a home office, intercut with close-ups of an automated money processing machine rapidly handling U.S. dollar bills. The segment concludes with a close-up of a financial screen displaying stock market data, including green and red numbers and a candlestick chart, underscoring market volatility influenced by these policies.

The graph shows a steady increase from 2001 to 2009, followed by a sharp rise after 2019, reaching nearly $9 trillion by 2021. The scene cuts to a suburban house with a 'FORECLOSURE' sign, symbolizing economic distress, then shifts to a busy trading floor where traders are intensely focused on screens and phones, highlighting market activity. segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a digital financial screen displaying cryptocurrency trading data, including green upward-trending graphs and ticker symbols like ADA/THB and IOST, as an unidentified female voice discusses trying to get 'more economic activity.' This transitions to a static shot of U.S. Savings Bonds, emphasizing traditional financial instruments, while an unidentified male voice explains that 'investors buy bonds to generate a modest but guaranteed return.' The scene then shifts to Roger W. Ferguson Jr., a bald man with glasses, wearing a maroon sweater over a white shirt and tie, seated in a professional setting with a bookshelf behind him, speaking directly to the camera. He states that 'US Treasury bonds are perhaps the safest assets out there,' known as a 'riskless asset,' and describes the 'very, very deep and liquid market' for 'conforming mortgages' that the Fed buys. Following this, an animated graphic appears, showing a horizontal bar divided into 'BONDS' (yellow) and 'STOCKS' (teal), with a U.S. Treasury seal above it. The bar dynamically shifts to show the Treasury’s allocation favoring bonds, then splits into two bars—one for the government and one for an individual—illustrating differing investment allocations. During this animation, Greg McBride, a man in a blue shirt and patterned tie, standing in a home office with a bookshelf, globe, and framed photos, speaks to the camera, recommending that 'many traditional investors' use a portfolio that 'balances these bonds against stocks.' He continues, explaining that 'when the Federal Reserve steps into the market, it's taking these safe bonds, making profits on them fall for everyone.' The animation concludes with the individual’s bar shifting toward stocks, while the unidentified female voice returns, explaining that from an 'investor perspective,' it's 'more likely that they are ideally going to be putting their capital to work in ways that support private innovation.' The video then cuts to a Casper Sleep showroom with a large branded box, a bed setup, and on-screen text reading 'Online mattress seller Casper Sleep files to go public,' dated January 10, 2020. During this final visual, Greg McBride's voice continues, stating that holding 'such a large balance sheet of nearly $9 trillion has contributed to this environment where a lot of money is flowing into risk assets,' leading to 'some crazy things' from companies that 'really don't have...' before the audio cuts off.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a Casper Sleep showroom, featuring a large branded box and beds, with on-screen text announcing 'Online mattress seller Casper Sleep files to go public' dated January 10, 2020. A male voice explains how businesses were able to go the IPO route in 2020 and 2021 to raise a lot of money. The scene transitions to a blue-lit Casper storefront with text stating 'Casper is going private again, at a quarter of its original IPO price' dated November 15, 2021, as the male voice continues, noting that "those businesses ultimately failed, there's going to be a lot of investors kind of left holding the bag." This is followed by a close-up of a stock ticker on a building facade, displaying red downward arrows and falling numbers, symbolizing market decline, while a second male voice states that "markets have come to rely on the Fed's purchasing patterns." The video then cuts to a Woman in striped top, seated in a cozy room, speaking directly to the camera, explaining that "by making the Federal Reserve so central in the efforts to get money to companies in the spring of 2020 and the summer of 2020, we did create an overall environment where we did far more to to backstop some really fragile financial intermediaries." She continues her commentary as the scene briefly shifts to a golden-hued cityscape at sunrise, then returns to her, where she adds that a large company's "ability to raise money by issuing new debt over the past couple years has just been astounding." The narrative progresses with a close-up of a U.S. $50 bill being processed by an automated money processing machine, as the second male voice states, "The Central Bank took on non-traditional assets, like securitized mortgage loans." This is followed by a 'SOLD' real estate sign in front of a suburban home, with the second male voice concluding, "To some, this has been controversial." The final segment shows archival footage from CNBC Business News dated February 19, 2009, depicting a chaotic NYSE trading floor with traders in suits and jackets, gesturing and reacting to screens, capturing the intensity of a financial downturn, while an agitated male voice from the archival clip shouts, "How many of you people want to pay for your neighbor's mortgage that has an extra bathroom and can't pay?"

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with archival CNBC Business News footage from February 19, 2009, capturing a chaotic NYSE trading floor where traders in suits and jackets intensely gesture and react to financial data on monitors, accompanied by shouts like "raise their hand" and "President Obama are you listening?" A wider view of the bustling NYSE floor with digital screens transitions as a male voiceover explains that the Federal Reserve's warning to wind down its balance sheet sent day traders into a panic. The scene then cuts to 'Kudlow's Corner' from June 20, 2013, where Larry Kudlow, a man in a suit and glasses, and a woman in a dark blazer discuss market sell-offs and the dollar’s rally. Kudlow's distinct, assertive voice states, "In the next year or sooner, we are going to end quantitative easing. We are going to end bond buying... That ain't bullish for gold." Following this, Greg McBride, a man in a blue shirt and patterned tie, appears in a home office setting with a bookshelf and globe, speaking directly to the camera. McBride, with a clear, explanatory voice, elaborates on the market's reaction: "The idea that one of the biggest buyers... would all of a sudden stop being a buyer... that caused investors to to freak out." He continues, explaining how the Fed later slowly allowed maturing bonds to roll off the balance sheet. The final shot displays a line graph titled 'Federal Reserve System balance sheet trends,' illustrating a sharp increase from 2008 to 2022, as the voiceover concludes, "Over time, emotions calmed, and the balance sheet plateaued. Ben Bernanke..."

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a dark blue line graph titled 'Federal Reserve System balance sheet trends,' illustrating a sharp increase in the balance sheet from 2002 to 2022, with a notable spike after 2020, sourced from the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis. A calm, informative male narrator explains how stock valuations were at a record high and the central bank started to unwind its balance sheet slowly before warning signs in 2019, noting that strong market conditions in the late 2010s gave the Fed confidence to let bonds mature. The scene then transitions to a press conference where a man in a suit stands at a podium with the Federal Reserve seal, addressing journalists with American and Federal Reserve flags in the background. This man, Greg McBride, speaks with a clear, analytical tone, detailing how economic growth slowed sharply in mid-2019, leading to interest rate cuts when unemployment was at a 50-year low, just before COVID. The video cuts to Greg McBride, identified as Senior Vice President and Chief Financial Analyst at Bankrate.com, speaking directly to the camera from his home office, as the narrator describes the pandemic bringing another significant round of bond buying, with the Fed taking safe Treasury bonds and mortgage-backed securities off the market, and setting up lending facilities to buy bonds from municipalities and corporations. A bustling city street with pedestrians is briefly shown, followed by a close-up of an automated money processing machine rapidly handling stacks of U.S. dollar bills. Roger W. Ferguson Jr., Former Vice Chair of the Federal Reserve, then speaks with a calm, authoritative voice, remarking that this was 'a new thing' the Fed did. An aerial shot of the Federal Reserve building is displayed before returning to Roger W. Ferguson Jr. The narrator then explains how the 2020 bond purchasing program brought investors flooding back into the stock market after a sudden collapse. The video then shows a financial data screen with rapidly changing stock tickers and numbers in green and red, before cutting back to Greg McBride, who concludes that holding such a large balance sheet of nearly $9 trillion has contributed to the current environment. The segment ends with a close-up of a financial data screen displaying green upward-trending graphs, cryptocurrency tickers like ADA/THB and IOST, and volume indicators such as 'Vol: 17.3M'.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with Greg McBride, a man in a blue shirt and patterned tie, speaking directly to the camera from his home office, explaining how an ultra-low rate environment drove money into "risk assets" and "crazy things" like cryptocurrency and NFTs. This is visually reinforced by a Bitcoin ATM with a large orange symbol and a 'CRYPTO' graphic over a pixelated person. McBride continues, noting that large cash injections boosted corporations at the expense of smaller businesses. The scene then transitions to a dynamic animation of a cityscape made of $100 bills, symbolizing financial markets, before cutting to a closed storefront with a 'WE WILL BE CLOSED UNTIL FURTHER NOTICE' sign, visually underscoring the struggles of small businesses. Kathryn Judge, a professor at Columbia Law School, then appears in a black-and-white striped top from a room with bookshelves, articulating how the Fed lacked the right tools to help small and mid-sized businesses, even with initiatives like the Main Street lending facility. She contrasts this by stating that the largest companies in the country were much more able to raise funds through mechanisms like issuing debt into public markets, which were bought up by open-end bond funds and ETFs. This point is illustrated by exterior shots of an Amazon building, a Wells Fargo branch, and an Exxon gas station, representing these large corporations. The segment concludes with a bustling New York Stock Exchange trading floor, displaying screens with stock market data, including a graphic showing 'STOCKS FALL 7% MINUTES AFTER S&P FALLS 7%', highlighting the volatility and activity in the public markets Judge described.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a chaotic scene on the NYSE trading floor, where a large screen displays a dramatic market sell-off with the S&P 500 down 7%, and a ticker reads 'STOCKS HALTED FOR 15 MINUTES AFTER S&P FALLS 7%'. Traders in dark jackets, including one with 'GTS' and '819' on the sleeve, are seen reacting to the volatility. Kathryn Judge, a woman with dark hair in a black-and-white striped top, then appears, speaking directly to the camera about the financial systems potentially grinding to a halt due to a lack of liquidity, warning against forcing a recession from a breakdown in the financial system. A narrator then explains that members of the Federal Reserve contend that emergency asset purchases are necessary, believing that debts will be paid as they mature. Roger W. Ferguson Jr., a bald man in a maroon sweater, is then shown speaking about how almost every crisis leads to a survey, commission, or hearing, after which Congress decides how to adjust authority. Jerome Powell, a man with gray hair and glasses in a dark suit, is seen at a formal hearing, gesturing as he speaks about focusing on these crises. Michelle Bowman, a woman with shoulder-length brown hair in a dark blazer, is shown speaking at a podium during the American Bankers Association event on February 21, 2022, with her title 'Member, Board of Governors, Federal Reserve' displayed, stating that the resulting end of pandemic asset purchases will remove another source of unneeded economic stimulus and expecting these steps to contribute to an easing in inflation pressures in the coming months. Roger W. Ferguson Jr. reappears, noting that certainly some people on the committee are eager to begin reducing the balance sheet. A market update graphic from CNBC Business News on January 5, 2022, briefly shows the Dow, NASDAQ, and S&P 500 with mixed performance. A narrator then details the Fed's plan to unwind its asset portfolio at a more aggressive pace than what it attempted following the housing crash. The scene shifts to an exterior shot of the Federal Reserve building under a clear blue sky, followed by a close-up of hands counting U.S. dollar bills at a desk. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Roger W. Ferguson Jr., a bald man with glasses, wearing a maroon sweater over a white shirt and tie, seated in a professional setting with a wooden bookshelf behind him. He speaks directly to the camera, explaining in a clear male voice how interest rates gradually rise from historically low levels, noting that mortgage and corporate borrowing rates are already higher. The scene transitions to an aerial view of a suburban neighborhood with newly built houses, one featuring a 'SOLD' sign, followed by a sweeping aerial shot of a modern cityscape. A male narrator's voice then states that 'The Fed will shrink its bond portfolio by two to three trillion dollars in this round,' as a financial data screen with a large red arrow and '$2-3 trillion' text appears. The narrator continues, warning that 'Market turbulence could follow the Fed's tightening of the economy, sparking a recession,' while the bustling New York Stock Exchange trading floor, filled with traders in dark 'GTS' jackets, is shown. Next, Kathryn Judge, a woman with dark hair in a black-and-white striped top, is seen in a cozy room with bookshelves and a couch, speaking directly to the camera. She articulately questions in a female voice, 'Are we going to go back to the Fed having a balance sheet of the size that it was in 2006 and early 2007?' and expresses skepticism, stating, 'The role of reserves on bank balance sheets has changed a lot.' Roger W. Ferguson Jr. returns, continuing his explanation with expressive hand gestures, asserting that the balance sheet is 'a new tool' being used 'quite consistently' and 'at a scale that was not imagined before.' The segment concludes with a CNBC logo transition screen, displaying a graphic titled 'Can the Federal Reserve control inflation?' featuring an image of a U.S. Treasury check.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a CNBC promotional graphic asking 'Can the Federal Reserve control inflation?', featuring a U.S. Treasury check with the Statue of Liberty, accompanied by light, slightly suspenseful electronic background music. This transitions to an automated money processing machine rapidly handling stacks of U.S. dollar bills, then a close-up of hands counting cash, all set to the same background music. A headline graphic appears, announcing 'U.S. consumer prices jump 6.2% in October, the biggest inflation surge in more than 30 years,' attributed to Jeff Cox, dated November 10, 2021, as a male narrator with a clear American accent explains that "Prices for just about everything are rising fast. In October 2021, inflation took its biggest jump in more than 30 years. It's hitting specific parts of the economy hardest." The visual shifts to a gas station price sign showing high fuel costs (e.g., $6.79 for Supreme) and a person in a mask pumping gas, while the narrator details that "Drivers face a 59% increase at the pump compared to one year ago." An aerial shot of a crowded car lot follows, with the narrator adding that "The average used vehicle is selling for 26% more than it was a year ago," before an aerial view of a coastal town with luxury homes is shown, as he notes, "Vacation homes are renting out at a premium too." Valerie Wilson, Director of the Program on Race, Ethnicity, and the Economy at the Economic Policy Institute, appears in a professional setting with a plant in the background, stating emphatically, "Nobody likes inflation. Nobody wants to pay higher prices for anything really." The scene then displays a line graph titled 'Personal consumption expenditures' illustrating a sharp rise in inflation rates (Trimmed mean and PCE) from 2021–2022, exceeding the 'Fed target rate' of 2%, sourced from the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, as the narrator explains, "Maintaining stable prices is one of the Federal Reserve's main responsibilities. In recent decades, the economy has hummed below the central bank's target rate." Finally, Mark Zandi, Chief Economist at Moody’s Analytics, is seen in his home office with a bookshelf, framed photos, and a background image of overdue bills and coins, speaking directly to the camera with a calm, authoritative American accent, stating, "Now, post-pandemic, the Fed, they want inflation, at least for a while, to be above 2%. And they'll get exactly what they want simply because of the acceleration and rank growth," before the narrator's voice briefly returns, "Critics say there are signs..." as the segment concludes.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of the United States Federal Reserve System seal on a dollar bill, as a male voiceover discusses "turmoil in the economy that the Fed isn't hearing." This transitions to Danielle DiMartino Booth, CEO and Founder of Quill Intelligence, in a red top, speaking in front of her branded backdrop, stating, "I think it's pretty darn clear that the Fed cannot control inflation on the downside or the upside, given the current experience." The scene shifts to a 'Mad Money' clip from November 16, 2021, showing Jim Cramer in a suit and pink tie, gesturing emphatically against a San Francisco backdrop, with on-screen text about Powell not needing to "slam the brakes on the economy." A male voiceover notes, "The Central Bank has its defenders too," before Jim Cramer emphatically declares, "The weight of the evidence is finally going Powell's way. Team Transitory is going to win!" Lawrence Mishel, Distinguished Fellow at the Economic Policy Institute, then appears in a home setting with framed art, speaking directly to the camera, explaining, "There's a lot of reasons to think that inflation is transitory. It doesn't mean it's going to be two months. It could be a year, but it's not going to be 4 or 5% a year for the next five years." A laptop screen displays Mishel’s video call on a wooden desk with a CNBC-branded mug and potted plants, as the male voiceover states, "In the backdrop, governments are spending big to keep society afloat." This is followed by a shot of masked and gloved volunteers packing food at a 'LOCAL FOOD DRIVE' event. The Federal Reserve building is shown in a wide exterior shot under a cloudy sky, then a close-up of its engraved facade, while the voiceover explains, "The US Treasury's debt is managed by the Fed. The bank's assets swelled as it printed trillions of dollars to backstop the country." An automated money processing machine then rapidly handles stacks of U.S. dollar bills, as a text overlay asks, 'Can the Federal Reserve control inflation?', and the voiceover poses the question, "And if so, what could it do to rein in the cost of living in the United States?" The segment concludes with a close-up of Jerome Powell, wearing glasses and a dark suit, thoughtfully pressing his fingers to his lips, as text overlays list 'Inflation basics', 'Expectations', 'The Phillips Curve', and 'Inflation targets'. A steady, slightly urgent background music track begins and continues as the video ends with a graphic displaying pie charts comparing PCE and CPI weights for various consumer categories, sourced from Haver Analytics and SG Cross Asset Research/Economics, while the voiceover explains, "The people who manage the US economy prefer to keep inflation around 2%. That's because a low and steady rate produces a healthy business environment."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a data visualization comparing PCE and CPI weights through two pie charts, highlighting categories like food, shelter, medical care, and transportation, as a narrator explains how components like food, energy, and housing are weighted to establish their importance. This transitions to a line graph titled 'Personal consumption expenditures,' showing inflation trends from 1960 to 2020, with lines for the Trimmed mean inflation rate and the Chain-type price index (PCE). The narrator introduces the Consumer Price Index (CPI) as tracking 93% of US spending, then describes the Trimmed mean inflation as focusing on core prices by throwing out outlier data, and finally introduces the PCE. The scene then cuts to Frank Nothaft, Chief Economist at CoreLogic, speaking in an office setting, who states, "The Fed really prefers to look at PCE, that is Personal Consumption Expenditures price index." The narration continues, explaining that the Fed's preferred PCE measure is broader than the trimmed mean but excludes energy and food data because prices take bigger swings in these industries more frequently, impacting the index's reliability. This explanation plays over a sequence of real-world visuals: the Federal Reserve building, an industrial facility, a well-lit grocery store aisle, a fresh produce section, a Hess gas station displaying high fuel prices, an aerial city view, and a street scene with American and Texas flags. The narration concludes by mentioning that some believe the PCE is flawed.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a Woman in red top, speaking directly to the camera in front of a 'Quill Intelligence' backdrop, her clear voice discussing the PCE's undercounting of housing as a major household expense. The scene transitions to aerial views of suburban and densely populated urban areas, followed by a return to the Woman in red top. A title card then appears over the Federal Reserve seal, announcing a key inflation gauge hitting a 30-year high, as a male narrator explains that in the fall of 2021, PCE numbers spiked, leading public officials to turn to the Fed for answers. Historical black-and-white footage of men in suits at a formal meeting, with an American flag in the background, illustrates the narrator's explanation of the Federal Reserve's original setup for a stable banking system and its expanded, century-long existence. Overlaid text clarifies the Fed's mandate: 'Maximize employment, Stabilize prices.' The narrator continues, stating Congress gave the Fed a dual mandate in 1977. Modern scenes then depict labor market dynamics with a 'HELP WANTED' sign in a pharmacy, a 'WE'RE HIRING! $500 SIGN-UP BONUS!' sign, and a long line of job seekers. The segment concludes with Valerie Wilson, Director at the Economic Policy Institute, speaking in a professional setting, explaining that part of the Fed's mandate is to maximize employment and stabilize prices by keeping inflation in check, with the narrator summarizing her point that the Fed's ability to manage inflation depends on the labor market.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute opens with Valerie Wilson, Director of the Program on Race, Ethnicity, and the Economy at the Economic Policy Institute, speaking directly to the camera in a professional setting. Wearing glasses, a patterned scarf, and a dark blazer, with a potted plant to her left, she articulately explains that current inflationary pressures stem from people shifting consumption from services to goods, causing demand to outpace supply, which suppliers haven't had adequate time to meet. A male narrator then takes over, describing how a sputtering global supply chain and backed-up ports are causing delays, as visuals show a long line of people waiting outside a building, followed by empty grocery store shelves emphasizing scarcity. An aerial view of a busy port with large container ships, including one labeled 'MAERSK,' and a massive yard filled with shipping containers, visually reinforces these global supply chain issues. The narrator continues, noting that many, including leaders of the Fed, believe the economy hasn't settled, and that Chair Powell is now walking back his previous use of the word 'transitory' to describe inflation. Jerome Powell, Chair of the Federal Reserve, is then seen speaking at a formal meeting on November 30, 2021, gesturing as he calmly states that it's probably a good time to retire that word and explain more clearly what they mean, implying it won't leave a permanent mark in the form of higher inflation. The narrator concludes by stating that the central bank believes current conditions don't change the long-term outlook, as inflation has actually been lower than what the Fed wanted in recent years. The segment ends with exterior shots of the Federal Reserve building, an American flag waving atop it, and a close-up of the Federal Reserve seal, reinforcing the institution's central role in these economic discussions.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of a MacBook Pro on a wooden desk, displaying a video call with Mark Zandi, Chief Economist at Moody’s Analytics, who, in a clear and articulate voice, discusses how pre-pandemic "inflation was soft" and below the Fed's 2% target. He continues, now in a full-screen view framed with a yellow border, explaining that post-pandemic, the Fed aims for inflation "above 2%" due to "acceleration in rent growth." A male narrator then takes over, stating that in 2019, "newly elected Chair Powell argued that long-term expectations of inflation were low," as Jerome Powell is seen in a suit and tie speaking at a formal podium with on-screen text about 'Expectations', 'The Phillips Curve', and 'Inflation targets'. The narrator continues, describing how "experts observing the labor market reported that the interest rate lift-off... cut the recovery short," while visuals show a line of job seekers bundled in winter clothing outside a UPS building and a drive-up COVID-19 testing site with medical staff in PPE. Powell is then shown again at a podium with a blue backdrop and flags, dated July 29, 2020, and his deep, authoritative voice is heard stating the need for "accommodative financial conditions." The narrator explains this means "dropping interest rates" to make prices "rise more quickly," as a customer inserts a card into a payment terminal at a grocery store checkout. Valerie Wilson, Director of the Program on Race, Ethnicity, and the Economy, then appears on a video call, her clear and direct voice asserting, "Nobody likes inflation. Nobody wants to pay higher prices for anything, really." The segment transitions to an 'Inflationary Psychology' title card over a shot of hands working with a calculator and documents, while the narrator concludes that "economists believe that expectations are the primary driver of inflation." Finally, the video returns to Mark Zandi, now with glasses and his name and title displayed on screen, who reiterates, "Well, people think inflation's gonna be high for a long time, they're going to say, 'Hey, Mr. employer...'

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a man in a dark suit and glasses, speaking directly to the camera from a home office, explaining how expectations of higher inflation lead to actual higher inflation, illustrating with a scenario where a businessman passes increased costs to consumers. As he speaks, the scene briefly transitions to a woman with white hair reviewing documents in a modern office before cutting back to the man, who emphasizes that people's future inflation views result in higher inflation, calling it 'the problem.' A vintage cartoon then appears, showing a cheerful butcher, accompanied by an old-timey male voiceover explaining that 'a wage raise means a corresponding rise in prices, unless productivity is increased proportionately,' followed by an animated male voice asking, 'What do you want? A guy with four arms?' The video then shifts to an exterior shot of the grand Federal Reserve building, as a male narrator introduces a paper by Jeremy B. Rudd. A close-up of the document, titled 'Why Do We Think That Inflation Expectations Matter for Inflation? (And Should We?)', is displayed, with the man in the suit interjecting that this is a 'non-consensus view.' The narrator continues, highlighting a critical quote from the paper, emphasized with a yellow highlight, stating that mainstream economics provides 'an apologetics for a criminally oppressive, unsustainable, and unjust social order.' The CNBC logo remains visible throughout.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a male narrator's voice discussing the wider movement of people who believe the Fed needs reform. This transitions to a close-up of Danielle DiMartino Booth, CEO and Founder of Quill Intelligence, wearing a bright red top and subtle jewelry, speaking directly to the camera from in front of her branded 'Quill Intelligence' backdrop. With engaged expressions and hand gestures, her clear, professional voice explains how an internal Fed debate from 2008-2010 questioned why they missed the financial and subprime crises. She asserts that the Fed's inflation model was 'broken' because it failed to incorporate securities and housing prices, which would have prevented them from being 'blindsided.' Booth states that despite writing white papers, the Fed did 'nothing' to fix this, instead using the flawed model to systematically understate inflation and justify easier monetary policy to prop up the stock market. As she speaks, the video cuts to a bustling NYSE trading floor, where traders in dark jackets with 'GTS' branding are intensely focused on screens and communicating, one holding a handheld device, amidst multiple monitors displaying financial data and 'NYSE' branding. The scene then transitions to a graphical representation of 'The Phillips Curve' on a dark blue background, showing a blue downward-sloping curve plotting 'Wage inflation' against 'Unemployment,' complete with scattered data points and a horizontal line for inflation targets, sourced from the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis and featuring CNBC branding. The male narrator's voice returns, explaining that many who watch the Fed cite breakdowns in models like the Phillips Curve, which economists use to make interest rate decisions.

segments.integrated_caption: The minute begins with a detailed graphical representation of 'The Phillips Curve' on a dark blue background, featuring a blue downward-sloping curve plotting 'Wage inflation' against 'Unemployment,' with scattered white data points and a horizontal line for inflation targets, sourced from the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis. A male narrator's voice explains the model, its inputs (inflation rates and employment data), and how various forces shift the economy along the curve. The graph animates with arrows, emphasizing the inverse relationship as the narrator details how a tight labor market shifts the curve to the left, indicating more job openings than available workers, which increases pressure on employers to raise wages, leading to higher inflation. He states the Fed can control inflation when it originates from the labor market. The scene then transitions to Valerie Wilson, a woman with dark hair, glasses, a patterned scarf, and a dark blazer, speaking directly to the camera from a professional setting. Her voice explains that the Federal Funds Rate is the Federal Reserve's main tool, and lowering this rate helps spur economic growth and job creation. While Valerie Wilson continues her commentary, explaining that raising the rate slows growth and job creation, and is done due to concerns about inflation growing too fast or potentially getting out of control because of unemployment, the video briefly cuts to Janet Yellen, a woman with white hair in a blue blazer, reviewing documents at a table, followed by Jerome Powell, a man with gray hair and glasses in a dark suit, speaking and gesturing. The scene also shows a trader on the NYSE floor, a man in a dark jacket with 'GTS' and '819' on the sleeve, focused on financial screens, before returning to Valerie Wilson for the conclusion of her remarks.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of Danielle DiMartino Booth, CEO and Founder of Quill Intelligence, wearing a bright red top and speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, in front of her branded 'Quill Intelligence' backdrop. She explains the tight relationship between inflation and wage inflation, leading to a spiral of rising wages, and how de-unionization and outsourcing to countries like India, which exported deflation due to cheaper labor, gave employers the upper hand over employees in America. She notes that this made the efficacy of the Phillips Curve outmoded, breaking the immediate feedback effect from rising prices into rising wages. The scene then transitions to a wider shot of her video call displayed on a MacBook Pro on a wooden desk, flanked by potted plants and a CNBC-branded mug, indicating a remote interview setup. segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with Lawrence Mishel, a man with white hair and a beard in a black t-shirt, speaking from a home setting with framed art and a bed visible behind him. As a narrator's voice explains that leaders are making adjustments to prepare for a 'new normal,' the visuals shift to a line of job seekers outside a building, some wearing hoodies and face masks, followed by a protest outside a McDonald’s where individuals hold signs advocating for higher wages and union support, with a large '$15' sign prominently displayed. The scene transitions to the White House exterior with a text overlay 'Inflation targets,' followed by President Joe Biden speaking at a podium flanked by officials and American flags. A narrator states that President Biden nominated Powell for a second term, hoping it would help the Fed maintain its independence, a statement confirmed by President Biden's own voice declaring, 'I'm nominating Jerome Powell.' The narrator adds that this will be important as the group embarks on a 'new and unusual decade.' The segment concludes with a man in a dark suit and glasses, speaking and gesturing from a home environment with framed photos behind him, beginning to discuss the strategy the Fed is now pursuing.

A line graph titled 'Federal funds effective rate' then appears, illustrating a sharp decline to near 0% by 2020, as a male narrator explains that the Fed has kept interest rates near zero for over a decade and will continue to do so because the US and other countries have failed to hit inflation targets. Next, Danielle DiMartino Booth, CEO and Founder of Quill Intelligence, in a red top, speaks expressively in front of her branded backdrop, asserting that "The Fed itself was incapable before of creating inflation," describing them as "pushing on a string," and explaining their intent to "allow inflation to run hot going forward" to compensate for years of failing to meet their 2% inflation target. The video then shows an aerial view of a large pink container ship labeled 'ONE' docked at a busy port with a city skyline, followed by nearly empty grocery store shelves, visually emphasizing supply shortages, as the narrator begins to discuss "temporary bottlenecks caused by the pandemic and its supply chain disruptions."

Catherine Mann, Global Chief Economist at Citi, is then seen in front of a large world map, quoting Milton Friedman that "inflation is always and everywhere a monetary phenomenon," and asserting that the "exploding" central bank balance sheets indicate impending inflation. The final segment shows B-roll footage of empty grocery store shelves with Perdue labels, shoppers wearing masks in a crowded supermarket, and a Walmart cashier scanning produce, while the female narrator discusses how supply shocks have driven up prices for some goods, though recent history suggests inflation is more likely to stay low.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Elizabeth Schulze, a CNBC Multimedia Reporter, seated in a bright, white kitchen-like setting with yellow flowers and a yellow tote bag. She explains in a clear, articulate female voice that when "high [unemployment] and consumer demand is weak, inflation is low," while on-screen text highlights the relationship between 'High unemployment' and 'Weak demand' equating to 'Low inflation.' She continues, detailing how "commodity prices" like oil can increase gas prices, and how "consumer and business expectations about prices" are another piece of the inflation puzzle. As she speaks, the scene transitions to an aerial view of a vast cornfield, followed by a desert oil field with pumpjacks and pipelines, and then a close-up of a red and white wind turbine blade, visually representing commodity production and energy. The narrative shifts to a retail environment, showing a clothing store with 'Customer Service' signage and sale racks, followed by a close-up of a hand selecting a yellow floral dress and then inserting a credit card into a payment terminal, illustrating consumer spending. Krishna Guha, a man with receding hair and glasses, then appears in a home office setting, his clear, articulate male voice explaining that "the level of actual inflation that we get will be pretty heavily influenced by the inflation rate that actors in the economy... expect to prevail." The scene returns to Elizabeth Schulze, who states that "Like many other central banks around the world, the Fed targets a 2% yearly inflation rate." A graphic appears with a steaming mug illustrating a '2% annual inflation rate,' showing a price increase from $2.00 to $2.04, as she explains that a "cup of coffee that costs $2 this year would cost $2.04 next year." She concludes by noting that "Central banks adjust their policies... to try to get to that 2% inflation level," as exterior shots of the Bank of England, the European Central Bank, and the Federal Reserve building are shown, emphasizing central banking institutions, and ending with a close-up of the 'FEDERAL RESERVE' engraving on the building’s marble facade.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Krishna Guha, Vice Chairman of Evercore ISI, speaking directly to the camera from his home office, seriously discussing the need to maintain enough inflation to allow for adjustments in fed funds rates over the business cycle. Elizabeth Schulze, a CNBC Multimedia Reporter, then appears in a bright, kitchen-like setting, calmly explaining that too much inflation devalues money, illustrating with an example of a $2 coffee costing $2.30 next year at a 15% inflation rate. This explanation is visually reinforced by a graphic of a steaming mug showing the coffee price increase, followed by an aerial view of a crowded car lot and a close-up of a red car's taillight, where the same 15% inflation rate graphic shows a $10,000 car costing $11,500 next year. Olivier Blanchard, Former Chief Economist at the IMF, then appears in his home office, thoughtfully stating that a very high inflation rate makes saving calculations extremely difficult. Following this, Jerome Powell, Federal Reserve Chairman, is shown in a formal setting from May 29, 2020, calmly stating that inflation concerns and risks are currently to the downside, with prices moving down due to businesses cutting prices. Elizabeth Schulze reappears, now accompanied by an 'Inflation 101' graphic outlining 'Weak demand,' 'Money printing,' and 'Risks,' as she explains that lockdowns have already depressed US prices due to cautious consumer spending in an uncertain economy. The segment concludes with a CNBC news graphic from June 10, 2020, showing the 'MAY CONSUMER PRICE INDEX' at 0.1% change, alongside stock market data, with a gesturing man on screen, all while the CNBC logo remains visible.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a CNBC financial news segment displaying a 'FAIR VALUE' graphic with stock market data for the S&P 500, Dow Jones, and NASDAQ, alongside a 'BREAKING NEWS' ticker reporting CPI ex-food & energy at 0.4% versus an estimated 0.2%. A male voice declares the "biggest drop in headline inflation since 1947," followed by another male voice detailing "Energy commodities down 20% with a 20% decline in gasoline. Fuel oil down 15%." A man in a white shirt and teal tie, seated in a home office, appears to be analyzing this data. The scene transitions to a grocery store checkout where a cashier in a blue uniform and face mask scans items, followed by a close-up of a person in a plaid shirt slicing vegetables in a kitchen, as a female voice explains, "There have been pockets of inflation in some areas like groceries, as more people cook at home." The video then cuts to an aerial view of a port with container ships and cranes, followed by a close-up of a medical ventilator screen displaying vital signs, as the female voice continues, "Disruptions in global trade from the virus have also raised prices for goods like medical supplies," concluding, "Still, these supply shocks haven't offset overall weak demand." A street view then shows a restaurant with outdoor seating and a 'GRAND OPENING' sign, while a different female voice discusses "the average person's seat," mentioning "grocery stores and and that sort of thing." The scene shifts to a woman with short blonde hair and glasses, wearing a gray blazer, speaking in front of a world map labeled 'THE WORLD', asserting, "The idea that there's going to be an outbreak of inflation, you know, 4%, 5%. That is just not on the horizon." This is followed by a Walmart grocery store aisle with shoppers pushing carts, and a checkout counter where a cashier in a teal shirt and mask scans produce. The initial female voice returns, explaining that "Many economists and policymakers expect wages will stay low as unemployment remains high." The video then shows a person in a white sweater placing items into an orange plastic bag. A blurred shot of the New York State Department of Labor building appears with a CNBC headline: 'Weekly jobless claims rise by more than 1 million for the 17th straight week', as the female voice adds, "Meanwhile, people are saving instead of spending their cash out of fear the economy could get worse." This is followed by a close-up of a hand using a smartphone displaying a headline: 'U.S. savings rate hits record 33% as coronavirus causes Americans to stockpile cash, curb spending'. The final scenes include an aerial view of Washington D.C. with the Washington Monument, overlaid with text 'Inflation 101: Money printing', and a close-up of an automated money processing machine handling U.S. dollar bills. Upbeat, slightly dramatic background music begins as a new female voice explains, "To try to boost the economy, policymakers in Washington have pumped trillions of dollars into the financial..." before the audio cuts off.

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with a close-up of hands counting stacks of U.S. $100 bills, visually emphasizing the physical handling of cash, while on-screen text from CNBC highlights 'Money printing' and 'Inflation 101'. A male voiceover explains that economic theories suggest "all this money printing could create the risk of inflation." This transitions to a vintage photograph of an older man with glasses and a suit, smiling, as the voiceover attributes the quote "Economist Milton Friedman famously said that if there's too much money in the economy chasing too few goods, prices will rise" to him, which is also displayed as on-screen text. Archival footage of bustling 1980s city streets, filled with pedestrians in period clothing and yellow taxis, illustrates the era as the voiceover recounts how "When inflation was surging in the 1980s, Fed Chairman Paul Volcker put Friedman's theory to the test... Volcker slowed the growth of money going into the economy and raised interest rates to tame inflation." Elizabeth Schulze, a Multimedia Reporter for CNBC, then appears in a bright, white kitchen-like setting with yellow flowers and a yellow tote bag, speaking directly to the camera, stating that "economists say there's been a break in the link between money creation and inflation in recent years, as the banking system has become more complex." Next, Catherine Mann, Global Chief Economist at Citi, wearing a gray blazer and glasses, delivers her insights in front of a large world map labeled 'THE WORLD,' explaining that "The rise of the financial system and... the diversification of the financial system is one of the reasons why... the Milton Friedman view of the world really is not as applicable, particularly in the United States, as it was in an earlier time." The segment concludes with an animated graphic displaying the Federal Reserve seal and a grid of dollar signs, some turning gold to symbolize inflationary pressure, as the male voiceover clarifies that "when the central bank prints money today, most of it isn't in the form of physical dollar bills."

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with an animated graphic on a dark blue background, depicting a circular flow of money (dollar signs in blue and yellow circles) moving between stylized bank buildings, symbolizing financial circulation. The animation evolves, showing the money flowing inward toward a central ring of smaller dollar signs, then outward again, illustrating a dynamic economic system. A female narrator explains that the Fed creates electronic money, using that electronic cash to buy assets and lend to banks, injecting money into the banking system, and utilizing so-called 'primary dealers' – a group of around two dozen big banks and brokerage firms that trade bonds. This transitions to Krishna Guha, Vice Chairman of Evercore ISI, speaking via video call from a home office with neutral walls and track lighting. He wears a light blue button-down shirt and glasses, clarifying that when the Fed creates money, it strictly creates central bank money or reserves, which are held by the banking system, and banks then decide what they're willing to lend out into the economy. The video then returns to the animated graphic, now showing the money flowing downward toward three blue human figures, symbolizing distribution to individuals. The narrator continues, stating that even if the Fed is pumping a lot of money into banks, the money won't reach the hands of consumers until banks lend it out. A wide shot of the U.S. Capitol building follows, with a runner crossing the street in the foreground. A CNBC news graphic appears over the Capitol, stating 'Congress passes plan to send taxpayers $1,200 checks in coronavirus aid' by Laura Koenig, dated March 25, 2020. The scene shifts to Elizabeth Schulze, a Multimedia Reporter for CNBC, seated in a bright, white kitchen-like setting with yellow flowers and a yellow tote bag in the background. She explains that this cash infusion still might not result in inflation, as most Americans needed the checks to make day-to-day payments and to make up for lost income during the crisis, not to go out and spend lavishly on other purchases. The final shot is of Catherine Mann, Global Chief Economist at Citi, speaking via video call in front of a large world map labeled 'THE WORLD', beginning to state, 'I think of them as more...'

segments.integrated_caption: The video opens with Catherine Mann, Global Chief Economist at Citi, a woman with short blonde hair and glasses in a gray blazer, speaking directly to the camera in front of a world map. She discusses "life preservers" preventing the economy from falling into a "deeper hole" due to the Covid crisis, noting they "don't represent stimulus yet." The scene transitions to an animated dark blue graph titled 'Federal Reserve total assets,' showing a yellow line sharply increasing post-2020, overlaid with 'Personal consumption expenditures' in cyan, with a blue circle highlighting the 2008-2012 period. A female voice, later identified as Elizabeth Schulze, explains that "recent history suggests that all the fiscal and monetary stimulus during the pandemic is unlikely to increase prices for consumers," adding that "when the Fed bought trillions of dollars of assets after the 2008 financial crisis, inflation never surged." Next, Gary Shilling, a bald Financial Analyst in glasses and a dark blazer, appears in a home setting, stating that after the "great recession," there was a conviction that "fiscal and monetary stimuli were going to result in huge inflation," leading many to "go to gold," but "inflation has come down." Krishna Guha, a man with receding hair and glasses in a light blue shirt, then speaks from a home office, asserting that "the experience of the last decade is that central bank balance sheet expansion certainly need not generate a period of excess inflation," and that "even with a big balance sheet, it's still be hard to get the inflation that you want." The segment concludes with Elizabeth Schulze, a Multimedia Reporter for CNBC, in a bright kitchen-like setting with yellow flowers, wearing a sleeveless blue top, as she begins to discuss the "limits to what history can teach us" regarding understanding economic trends, with on-screen text listing 'Inflation 101,' 'Weak demand,' 'Money printing,' and 'Risks.'

On-screen text highlights 'Inflation 101', 'Weak demand', 'Money printing', and 'Risks'. The scene then transitions to Catherine Mann, Global Chief Economist at Citi, who, in front of a large world map labeled 'THE WORLD', articulately distinguishes between consumer price inflation and asset price inflation, emphasizing the significant increases in the latter with expressive hand movements. Elizabeth Schulze's voice returns as a voiceover, stating that 'Inflation expectations are another risk,' as the visuals shift to an exterior shot of the New York Stock Exchange with an American flag and a 'TOGETHER, WE’RE STRONG.' banner, followed by a close-up of the 'Fearless Girl' statue wearing a face mask, and a quick shot of hands counting U.S. dollar bills on an automated money processing machine. Finally, Olivier Blanchard, Former Chief Economist at the IMF, appears in a well-lit home office with bookshelves, speaking with a distinct accent about how expected inflation can lead to demands for higher wages and prices. Throughout the segment, the CNBC logo remains visible in the top right corner, maintaining a professional and informative tone.

segments.integrated_caption: The segment opens with a close-up of iPhones on display in a modern retail setting, with customers interacting, as a female voice, later identified as Elizabeth Schulze, begins to explain how factors like globalization, technology, and aging populations play a role in consumer prices. The scene quickly transitions to the bustling New York Stock Exchange floor, showing traders in blue jackets monitoring screens, followed by an aerial view of a busy port with container ships and cranes, visually reinforcing the discussion of global trade and supply chains. Schulze's voice continues, warning that a weaker US dollar or backlash against global supply chains, disrupted by the pandemic, could create inflation risks. Richard Bernstein, a bald man in a dark suit and glasses, then appears from a home office, his distinct male voice stating that sealing borders and embarking on huge monetary and fiscal stimulus could indeed create inflation. The narrative shifts to a wide shot of the U.S. Capitol building at golden hour, then a close-up of the American flag waving, as Elizabeth Schulze's voice returns, accompanied by light, investigative background music, to introduce 'record high public debt' as another big risk to inflation, noting that trillions in economic stimulus during the pandemic have rapidly increased government debt. President Joe Biden is then seen signing a document at a desk, flanked by officials, symbolizing government action related to this debt. A close-up of U.S. Savings Bonds is shown, followed by a CNBC title card stating, 'The U.S. is running a $1 trillion deficit, but politicians and investors don’t see a problem,' with the Capitol in the background, as Schulze's voice explains that some economists favor deficit spending for public investment, though the inflationary effects are debated. The segment concludes with Elizabeth Schulze, a CNBC Multimedia Reporter, seated in a bright, white kitchen-like setting with yellow flowers, speaking directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, her clear, informative voice explaining that fixed-dollar government debts make higher inflation an easier way to pay them off, with some worrying about political pressure on central banks to chase higher inflation before the audio cuts off.
